I'll rely on the well-established Meisl/Cohen/Knowles amyloid kinetics literature that I know: secondary nucleation saturates in monomer concentration (Michaelis-Menten form), and small-molecule inhibitors (like bexarotene) act by binding fibril surface catalytic sites, selectively delaying the early-time lag phase. The breakthrough idea aligns with this: a saturable autocatalytic term with inhibitor terms in the denominator that lengthen the early rise asymmetrically.
